critique.summary: The article discusses a critical bug in PSpice's AES-256 encryption implementation that drastically reduces the effective keyspace, enabling a brute-force attack to recover user keys in seconds. The author introduces SpiceCrypt, a tool that exploits this vulnerability to decrypt PSpice-encrypted SPICE model files, restoring interoperability with other simulators.
mainPoints: PSpice uses a proprietary encryption scheme with six modes; mode 4 incorporates user-supplied keys but has a key derivation bug.
The bug causes only a 4-byte portion of the key to be used in AES-256 encryption, reducing the keyspace from 2^256 to 2^32.
This reduction enables a brute-force attack using a known plaintext prefix to recover the user key quickly.
The root cause is likely a copy-paste error where the short key intended for DES was used for AES instead of the extended key.
SpiceCrypt is a released tool that implements this attack and supports decryption of multiple SPICE encryption formats.
Fixing the bug now would break compatibility with encrypted models created over the past twelve years.
critique.truthfulness: The technical details provided in the article align with established cryptographic principles and known vulnerabilities related to improper key handling and AES key sizes. The explanation of the bug, the reduced keyspace, and the brute-force attack using a known plaintext is plausible and consistent with cryptanalysis methods. The description of the tool SpiceCrypt and its capabilities is credible, and the timeline matches PSpice's AES-256 introduction in 2014. However, the article is authored by an individual researcher on a personal blog and does not cite independent sources or official statements from Cadence Design Systems, so external verification is limited.
considerations: The article does not include responses or acknowledgments from Cadence Design Systems regarding the bug or its impact.
Legal and ethical implications of decrypting proprietary encrypted files are briefly mentioned but not deeply explored.
Potential mitigations or workarounds for users relying on PSpice-encrypted models are not discussed.
The article assumes the reader has advanced technical knowledge of cryptography and reverse engineering.
No information is provided on whether newer versions of PSpice have addressed this vulnerability.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a cryptographic bug in PSpice's AES-256 encryption implementation, specifically a copy-paste error that reduces the effective keyspace drastically. Commenters highlight the insecurity of the encryption mode used and suggest the encryption was more for obfuscation than real security.

comments.author: jtsylve
comments.text:
I posted SpiceCrypt (https://github.com/jtsylve/spice-crypt) a few days ago for decrypting LTspice models. It now supports all six PSpice encryption modes as well.

PSpice is Cadence's SPICE simulator. Vendors encrypt component models with it, which locks them to PSpice and prevents use in NGSpice, Xyce, etc. Modes 0-3 and 5 derive keys entirely from constants in the binary, so those are straightforward once you extract them.

Mode 4 is the interesting one. It's the only mode with user-supplied key material and uses AES-256 in ECB mode. The key derivation has two base keys: a 4-byte short key (originally for DES) and a 27-byte extended key (intended for AES). The code passes only the short key to the AES engine -- it looks like a copy-paste from the DES path that was never corrected. The short key gets null-terminated and zero-padded to 32 bytes, so 28 of 32 AES key bytes are known. Effective keyspace is 2^32, brute-forceable in seconds with AES-NI.

The first encrypted block after every marker is a metadata header with a known plaintext prefix, which gives you a crib for validation. Once you recover the 4-byte short key, the full user key is also recoverable from the decrypted header.

This has likely been shipping since PSpice 16.6 in 2014. Fixing it would break every encrypted model created in the last twelve years.

The blog post linked above walks through the full details. The repo also has specifications documenting all the encryption schemes: https://github.com/jtsylve/spice-crypt/tree/v2.0.1/SPECIFICA...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, technical explanation of the bug, supported by references to the code and cryptographic principles, making it the most accurate and informative.

comments.author: pseudohadamard
comments.text:
TFA says it all in the first sentence describing the problem:

  The Bug

  Mode 4 uses AES-256 in ECB mode ...
ECB is the least secure encryption mode you can use, the one that's warned against in every beginner text. Seeing this is a bit like seeing "We vibe-coded our firewall in PHP...", it's pretty much a written guarantee that the rest of it will be a catalogue of wrong.

They did use AES-256 though, because using keys that go to 11 for your insecure encryption looks good in the marketing materials.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies the use of ECB mode as a major security weakness, reinforcing the critique of the encryption approach.

comments.author: alexchengyuli
comments.text: A 4-byte key and a 32-byte key both produce output that looks like ciphertext. Unlike most bugs, crypto bugs don't produce visible errors. That's why this one survived 12 years.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully explains why the bug went unnoticed for so long, though it is less technical than the top comments.

comments.author: userbinator
comments.text: This "encryption" was arguably never for any security anyway, just obfuscation.
comments.analysis: While somewhat speculative, this comment reflects a common viewpoint that the encryption was intended more as a deterrent than robust security.
